title: Fish and chips
subtitle: Beer-battered cod, thick-cut chips, fried twice
excerpt: Cod in a light beer batter, fried until the batter is shatteringly crisp, served with chips fried twice — once to cook through, once to crisp. The batter must be cold, the oil must be hot, and the chips must be double-fried. Homemade fish and chips is a weekend project rather than a quick meal.
sourceNotes: Fish and chips became Britain's national fast food in the second half of the 19th century, when railways brought cheap fish inland and the frying technique from the Jewish community in East London spread nationally. By 1910 there were over 25,000 fish and chip shops in Britain. The combination is now as much a cultural identifier as it is a food.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Blanch the chips a day ahead, dry, and refrigerate. First-fry them the day before for even better results.
recipe.temperatureNote: Oil at 140°C for chips first fry, 180°C for chips second fry and fish

content.text: Double-frying the chips is not optional for a chip that is crisp outside and fluffy inside. The first fry at 140°C cooks the potato through without colouring it. The second fry at 180°C crisps the surface in minutes. Rest the chips between fries so the outside dries.

The batter must be ice-cold when it hits the hot oil. Cold batter hitting hot oil creates a crisp, light shell from the steam differential. Warm batter produces a dense, greasy coating. Rest the batter in the fridge and use it straight from cold.
Make the batter and first-fry the chips

Whisk the {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and cold {{beer}} until smooth. Refrigerate. Parboil the {{potato}} chips in salted water for 6 minutes, drain, and dry thoroughly. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} to 140°C in a large deep pan or deep fryer. First-fry the chips in batches for 6 to 8 minutes until cooked through but not coloured. Drain on kitchen paper. Rest for at least 20 minutes (or refrigerate).
Second-fry the chips and fry the fish

Increase the oil to 180°C. Second-fry the chips in batches for 3 to 4 minutes until golden and crisp. Drain on kitchen paper and season with salt. Keep warm in a low oven while frying the fish. Pat the {{cod-fillet}} completely dry. Dust lightly in flour. Dip in the cold batter, letting any excess drip off. Fry in the 180°C oil for 6 to 8 minutes per side, until the batter is deep golden and the fish is cooked through. Drain on kitchen paper and serve immediately with the chips.

items.cause: Fish too wet, or not dusted in flour first
items.fix: Pat the fish completely dry before dusting in flour. The flour gives the batter something to adhere to

items.symptom: Batter greasy and dense rather than light and crisp
items.cause: Oil temperature too low, or batter too warm
items.fix: Check the oil temperature with a thermometer before frying each batch. Cold batter is essential — warm batter absorbs more oil

items.symptom: Chips soft and limp even after the second fry
items.cause: Not dried properly after parboiling, or oil temperature too low for the second fry
items.fix: Dry the chips on a clean tea towel or kitchen paper after parboiling. The surface must be dry before frying for the crust to form

content.text: Fish and chips is Britain's most recognised national dish internationally. It is a Friday evening and weekend meal, traditionally associated with the seaside. The homemade version requires more effort than collecting from a chip shop but produces a different result: a thicker, crispier batter and chips cut to your own specification.
